segments.text: Thanks for joining us. This is OmniView Episode 25. It's March 29, 2026, and as always, we're covering
what happened from every angle. Here's the news, the perspectives, and the questions worth asking.
Here's what's making news in the OmniView World today. Central banks around the world are
actively exploring and designing central bank digital currencies known as CBDCs. These digital
versions of sovereign money represent one of the most significant shifts in monetary architecture
and decades. The push has accelerated as physical cash usage continues its long decline in many
economies, while private digital payment systems from companies like Alipay, PayPal, and various
FinTech startups have captured growing shares of everyday transactions. The core concern for many
central banks is preserving monetary sovereignty, ensuring that as public money moves increasingly
onto private ledgers, the state retains its traditional role at the center of the monetary system.
Different institutions frame this challenge in distinct ways. The bank for international settlements
and many G7 central banks describe CBDCs as a way to ensure the public continues to have access
to a risk-free central bank liability in digital form, maintaining the anchor of trust that physical
cash has provided for generations. Emerging market central banks often emphasize financial inclusion,
pointing out that large portions of their populations remain unbanked and that digital currencies
could reduce the high costs of remittances and domestic payments that currently burden families
and small businesses. Some more skeptical analysts argue the real driver is the desire to maintain control
over the monetary system as technology erodes the effectiveness of cash, which has historically
operated outside direct surveillance. What's interesting here is how the motivation for CBDCs varies
significantly depending on a country's economic structure and stage of development. In advanced
economies with sophisticated financial systems, the conversation often centers on maintaining relevance
and countering private sector innovation. In developing nations, the focus tends to be more on practical
improvements in access and cost reduction. The question worth considering are central banks primarily
solving a genuine problem of monetary sovereignty or are they responding to technological change in ways
that could expand state influence over money? The answer may differ substantially depending on which
part of the world you're examining. Shifty. Nung now to the technical design choices that will
determine what CBDCs actually become. These aren't abstract engineering decisions that they will shape
how people experience money in their daily lives for decades to come. CBDCs come in two primary
flavors, wholesale and retail. Wholesale CBDCs are restricted to financial institutions for settling
large value payments and are relatively uncontroversial with several countries already operating similar
systems that function like upgraded versions of existing central bank reserves. Retail CBDCs available
to households and businesses for everyday use are where the real design battles occur. Within retail
CBDCs, systems can be account-based resembling online banking with stronger identity verification
requirements or token-based functioning more like digital cash with greater potential for anonymity.
Another key choice is the two-tier model preferred by most central banks where commercial banks and
authorized payment providers handle customer onboarding and relationship management while the central
bank issues the actual currency. China's digital yuan largely follows this two-tier approach in practice
despite being legally a direct claim on the central bank. The third critical choice involves privacy
architecture ranging from full anonymity for small transactions to complete traceability with many
projects exploring tiered privacy using advanced cryptography like zero-knowledge proofs. Perspectives
on these designs differ sharply. Supporters of account-based systems argue they make monetary policy
tools like programmable money more feasible and align better with existing anti-money laundering
frameworks. Advocates for token-based systems emphasize preserving cash-like properties and
individual privacy that many citizens have come to expect from physical currency. The question worth
considering which technical features should be prioritized when the choice is made now will be difficult
to reverse in the coming decades. Once millions of people and businesses begin using a particular
CBDC design, changing course becomes enormously expensive and disruptive. These decisions being made today
in committee rooms and pilot programs may lock in the character of money for the rest of the century.
Next, let's examine the monetary policy and financial stability implications of these designs.
This is where the technical choices meet real economic consequences. Proponents argue CBDCs could
strengthen monetary policy transmission by allowing central banks to pass interest rates directly
to the public, including negative rates in theory, something that's proven difficult with physical cash.
They also see potential for programmable money that could enable more targeted fiscal stimulus,
such as funds that expire after a certain period or can only be spent on specific categories of goods
during economic downturns. Critics from the banking sector warn that CBDCs could lead to disintermediation,
wherein a crisis depositors rapidly shift funds from commercial banks to the perceived safety
of central bank digital money. This could create digital bank runs that spread faster than
traditional ones, according to these concerns, potentially amplifying financial instability rather
than reducing it. Central banks counter that they can design safeguards like holding limits,
fees on large balances or interest rate tiers to prevent such runs and maintain the important role
commercial banks play in credit creation. There is also debate about whether CBDCs would crowd out
private innovation or complement it by providing a neutral settlement asset that all participants
can trust. Free market voices argue any central bank digital currency will inevitably distort
competition and become a de facto standard that private solutions must work around.
What's interesting is how these design features could either stabilize or destabilize the financial
system, depending on how they are implemented, a poorly designed CBDC might exacerbate bank
runs during stress, while a thoughtfully constructed one could actually enhance resilience.
The question worth considering, can central banks design CBDCs that enhance policy effectiveness
without creating new vulnerabilities in the banking sector during times of stress?
The tension between innovation and stability runs through this entire debate.
Now turning to the highly polarized debate around privacy, surveillance, and civil liberties,
this has become perhaps the most emotionally charged aspect of the CBDC discussion.
Civil liberties organizations and some cryptocurrency advocates worry that CBDCs create technical
infrastructure for unprecedented financial surveillance and programmable control over individual spending.
They point to potential features like transaction, expiration, merchant restrictions,
or automatic tax collection as tools that could be abused by governments in the future,
especially during times of social or political tension.
Central banks and regulators respond that no major CBDC project is currently proposing such extensive
controls and that cash is already being displaced by traceable private systems like credit cards
and mobile payment apps which operate under existing anti-money laundering and know your customer rules.
Privacy advocates counter that placing the ledger directly with the central bank changes the
incentives and removes friction between government and financial data that currently exists when
information must be requested from private companies. Many projects are exploring selective disclosure
using techniques like zero knowledge proofs, though these remain technically challenging at population
scale and may involve difficult trade-offs between privacy and functionality. The question worth
considering does moving from private to central bank ledgers fundamentally alter the balance between
privacy and oversight even with cryptographic safeguards in place. The technical possibilities are
still evolving but the philosophical stakes are clear. Let's look at the geopolitical and international
dimensions of CBDC development. What began as a domestic monetary policy conversation has increasingly
taken on strategic significance. CBDCs are now viewed through the lens of great power competition
with China's progress on its digital yuan being closely watched in Washington and Brussels.
The United States has taken a more cautious approach with the Federal Reserve emphasizing research
rather than rapid deployment partly due to concerns about implementation risks and the dollar's
current dominant position. The European Union is advancing its digital euro project with a
strong focus on privacy and strategic autonomy from both American tech giants and Chinese payment
systems. Cross-border projects like the one involving China, Hong Kong, Thailand, and the UAE
explore using CBDCs for faster, cheaper international settlements that could bypass some traditional
correspondent banking channels. Perspectives differ on whether these efforts represent healthy
competition that could lower costs for consumers and businesses or a deliberate attempt to reduce
reliance on the dollar-based system that has dominated global finance since World War II.
Developing countries see CBDCs as a way to leapfrog outdated infrastructure, reduce dollar
dependence in remittances and combat illicit finance more effectively. Others in these nations worry
about technological capacity, implementation costs, and the risk of becoming dependent on foreign CBDC
systems or standards set by larger powers. What's interesting here is how monetary technology has become
another arena for strategic rivalry between major powers. The choices nations make about their
digital currencies may influence not just their domestic economies, but also their position in the
evolving global financial order. The question worth considering, will CBDCs ultimately reinforce
existing global financial hierarchies or create genuine alternatives for smaller economies?
The early evidence suggests both possibilities remain open depending on how these systems are
designed and governed. Now to really understand this entire discussion, there's something most
coverage leaves out. The reality is that CBDC design involves a series of deliberate trade-offs
rather than a simple choice between digital cash and surveillance money. Each technical decision
embeds assumptions about the proper relationship between citizens, banks, and the state. For example,
choosing strong privacy protections may limit the effectiveness of certain monetary policy tools
that policymakers believe could help stabilize the economy during downturns. Conversely,
designing for easy policy transmission may reduce the anonymity that people currently expect from
cash and have come to value in a digital age. The two-tier distribution model keeps commercial
banks involved but creates questions about who actually controls customer data and relationships.
Privacy architectures using cryptography sound promising but face real scalability challenges
when applied to entire populations with millions of daily transactions. No large economy has fully
launched a widely used retail CBDC yet, meaning these choices are still being actively debated
in pilot programs and research papers rather than proven in practice. The common misunderstanding is
treating all CBDCs as essentially the same when the design variations will determine their actual
impact far more than the simple fact of their existence. A retail CBDC with strict holding limits and
strong privacy features would function very differently from one designed for maximum policy flexibility
and extensive monitoring capabilities. Next time you hear claims about CBDCs, ask yourself which
specific design features are being discussed and which trade-offs are being ignored in the heat
of the debate. The devil, as they say, is very much in these technical details. Before we go,
tomorrow watch for any updates on how major central banks are adjusting their CBDC research priorities
in response to private sector payment innovations that continue to reshape how money moves. That's
omniview. For full source links and more context, check out today's written briefing on the omniview
summaries page. As always, compare outlets, look for primary documents, and separate what's known
from what's assumed. If balanced perspectives are valuable to you, share this with a friend and
subscribe wherever you listen. See you tomorrow. This podcast is curated by Patrick but generated
using AI voice synthesis of my voice using 11 labs. The primary reason to do this is I unfortunately
don't have the time to be consistent with generating all the content and wanted to focus on creating
consistent and regular episodes for all the themes that I enjoy and I hope others do as well.
